question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, under a market whose loanToken is a fee-on-transfer ERC20, call midnight.take() with a SetterRatifier-ratified buy offer such that SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) delivers fewer tokens than buyerAssets - sellerAssets, while claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] is incremented by the full buyerAssets - sellerAssets, causing the protocol's claimableSettlementFee accounting to exceed actual token balance and making fee claims revert or drain lender withdrawable funds? Preconditions: loanToken charges a 1% fee on every transfer; maker has ratified a root via setIsRootRatified; offer.buy == true; units > 0. Call sequence: maker.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> taker.take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker, receiver, address(0), '') -> SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, midnight, buyerAssets-sellerAssets) delivers (buyerAssets-sellerAssets)*0.99. Invariant tested: midnight.claimableSettlementFee(loanToken) <= loanToken.balanceOf(midnight) - midnight.withdrawable(id). Scoped impact: accounting corruption; insolvency of settlement fee reserve. Proof idea: deploy FeeOnTransferToken with 1% fee as loanToken; fuzz units and fee rate; assert claimableSettlementFee <= actual balance after take.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified sell offer where loanToken is fee-on-transfer, trigger take() such that SafeTransferLib.safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, sellerAssets) delivers sellerAssets*(1-fee) to receiver while sellerPos.credit is reduced by the full sellerAssets units, causing the seller's credit to be understated relative to actual tokens received and creating a permanent shortfall in withdrawable? Preconditions: loanToken charges fee; offer.buy == false; seller has existing credit; maker has ratified root. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker, receiverIfTakerIsSeller, address(0), '') -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, sellerAssets). Invariant tested: sum(creditOf(id, user) for all users) <= withdrawable(id). Scoped impact: insolvency; lender credit exceeds withdrawable. Proof idea: stateful fuzz with fee-on-transfer token; track sum of all credits vs withdrawable after each take.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take / onBuy callback] Can an unprivileged maker, whose buy offer is ratified via SetterRatifier and whose offer.callback implements IBuyCallback, deploy a callback that always reverts in onBuy(), causing every take() of that offer to revert after isRatified() succeeds and consumed is incremented, thereby permanently freezing the offer's consumption group and preventing any taker from filling any offer sharing that group? Preconditions: maker has ratified root; offer.callback is a contract whose onBuy always reverts; consumed[maker][group] is incremented before callback. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> taker.take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker, receiver, address(0), '') -> onBuy reverts -> entire take reverts -> consumed reverts (atomically). Invariant tested: if isRatified passes and consumed is within cap, take must not permanently revert due to maker-controlled callback. Scoped impact: fund freeze; takers cannot fill any offer in the group. Proof idea: deploy RevertingBuyCallback; assert take always reverts; verify no alternative path exists to fill the offer.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take / onSell callback] Can an unprivileged maker, whose sell offer is ratified via SetterRatifier and whose offer.callback implements ISellCallback, deploy a callback that returns bytes32(0) instead of CALLBACK_SUCCESS, causing take() to revert with WrongSellCallbackReturnValue after all position accounting (credit/debt/collateral) has been updated in storage, and verify that the revert correctly rolls back all state changes including consumed? Preconditions: offer.buy == false; offer.callback returns bytes32(0); maker has ratified root; position accounting is updated before sellerCallback is called. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take() -> position updates -> safeTransferFrom -> onSell returns bytes32(0) -> revert. Invariant tested: all state changes including consumed, credit, debt must be atomically reverted if callback returns wrong value. Scoped impact: fund freeze if callback is non-deterministically bad; accounting corruption if revert is partial. Proof idea: deploy InvalidSellCallback; assert all storage is unchanged after revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: setIsRootRatified / isRatified] Can an unprivileged operator, who has been authorized by maker via midnight.setIsAuthorized(operator, true, maker) for an unrelated purpose (e.g., collateral management), call setIsRootRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, maliciousRoot, true) to ratify a Merkle root containing offers the maker never intended, because setIsRootRatified only checks IMidnight.isAuthorized(maker, msg.sender) without any action or market scope, allowing the operator to enable arbitrary offer fills against the maker? Preconditions: maker has authorized operator for any purpose; operator constructs a Merkle tree with a malicious offer (e.g., maxUnits = type(uint256).max, tick = 0); operator calls setIsRootRatified. Call sequence: maker.setIsAuthorized(operator, true, maker) -> operator.setIsRootRatified(maker, maliciousRoot, true) -> attacker.take(maliciousOffer, ratifierData, units, attacker, ...). Invariant tested: ratifier approval must bind correct maker intent; coarse authorization must not allow unintended offer ratification. Scoped impact: gate bypass; unauthorized offer fill; direct loss to maker. Proof idea: unit test showing operator can ratify root with offer maker never signed.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker pass malformed ratifierData (e.g., empty bytes or truncated ABI encoding) to take() with a SetterRatifier-ratified offer, causing abi.decode(ratifierData, (bytes32, uint256, bytes32[])) to revert with a low-level ABI decode error rather than a clean RatifierFail, and does this revert propagate correctly through take() without leaving any partial state (consumed, credit, debt) committed? Preconditions: maker has ratified a valid root; taker passes ratifierData = hex'' or ratifierData = abi.encode(bytes32(0)) (missing fields). Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take(offer, hex'', units, taker, ...) -> isRatified -> abi.decode reverts. Invariant tested: malformed ratifierData must cause full revert with no partial state; consumed must not be incremented. Scoped impact: fund freeze if revert is partial; DoS if revert is unclean. Proof idea: fuzz ratifierData with random bytes; assert consumed[maker][group] unchanged after revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.isLeaf] Can an unprivileged taker supply a proof array of length >= 256 in ratifierData, causing HashLib.isLeaf to evaluate leafIndex >> proof.length == 0 as always true (since Solidity shifts uint256 by >= 256 bits to 0), bypassing the LeafIndexOutOfRange check, and then iterate the proof loop 256+ times causing gas exhaustion that DoS-es the take() call for any taker attempting to fill a SetterRatifier-ratified offer? Preconditions: maker has ratified a root; taker constructs ratifierData with proof.length = 256 and arbitrary leafIndex. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take(offer, abi.encode(root, leafIndex, proof256), units, taker, ...) -> isLeaf iterates 256 times -> gas exhaustion. Invariant tested: isLeaf must enforce proof.length <= 20 (or some gas-safe bound) to prevent DoS. Scoped impact: low-cost DoS; fund freeze for all takers of the offer. Proof idea: unit test with proof.length = 256; measure gas; assert revert or gas > block limit.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer where loanToken is an ERC777 token with a tokensToSend hook, trigger reentrancy into midnight.take() or midnight.withdraw() during the safeTransferFrom call inside take(), exploiting the fact that position accounting (credit, debt, consumed) is already committed before the transfer, to double-spend credit or extract more assets than entitled? Preconditions: loanToken is ERC777; taker's tokensToSend hook reenters midnight; position state is committed before safeTransferFrom. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take() -> position updates committed -> safeTransferFrom triggers tokensToSend -> reentrant take() or withdraw() -> second position update. Invariant tested: ERC20 transfer must not re-enter Midnight; credit/debt must not be double-counted. Scoped impact: direct loss; accounting corruption. Proof idea: deploy ERC777LoanToken with reentrant hook; assert credit/debt invariants hold after reentrant take.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: setIsRootRatified] Can an unprivileged attacker, who is not authorized by the maker, call setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) and have it succeed if IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(maker, attacker) returns true due to a non-standard or malicious MIDNIGHT address being set at SetterRatifier construction, or if the MIDNIGHT contract has a bug in isAuthorized that returns true for arbitrary callers? Preconditions: SetterRatifier is deployed with a MIDNIGHT address that has a buggy isAuthorized; attacker is not the maker. Call sequence: deploy SetterRatifier(buggyMidnight) -> attacker.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> isAuthorized returns true -> root is ratified. Invariant tested: only maker or maker-authorized addresses can ratify roots; MIDNIGHT immutability must be verified at deployment. Scoped impact: gate bypass; unauthorized offer ratification. Proof idea: deploy mock Midnight with isAuthorized always returning true; assert setIsRootRatified succeeds for attacker.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged maker ratify a root, then call setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) to deactivate it, and then in the same block have a taker's take() succeed because isRatified is called before the deactivation is processed, or conversely, can a maker front-run a taker's take() with a deactivation to cause the take to revert, effectively using SetterRatifier as a griefing mechanism to freeze takers who have already committed collateral? Preconditions: maker has ratified root; taker has deposited collateral and is about to call take(); maker front-runs with setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false). Call sequence: maker.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> taker.supplyCollateral() -> maker.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) [front-run] -> taker.take() -> isRatified fails with NotRatified. Invariant tested: takers must not be griefed by maker deactivation after collateral commitment; exit paths must remain available. Scoped impact: fund freeze; taker's collateral is locked with no way to borrow. Proof idea: simulate front-run with vm.prank; assert taker cannot take after deactivation.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, during a take() with a SetterRatifier-ratified offer whose maker has a callback (offer.callback), exploit the fact that the onBuy callback is called after consumed is incremented but before the health check, to reenter setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) from within the callback, deactivating the root mid-take, and does this affect the current take's isRatified result (which was already checked) or any subsequent nested take? Preconditions: offer.callback is a contract that calls setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) in onBuy; maker has authorized the callback contract. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified(true) -> take() -> isRatified passes -> consumed incremented -> onBuy callback -> setIsRootRatified(false) -> callback returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS -> take completes. Invariant tested: root deactivation during callback must not affect current take; subsequent takes must correctly fail. Scoped impact: accounting corruption if root state affects in-flight take. Proof idea: deploy ReentrantDeactivateCallback; assert current take succeeds; assert subsequent take fails.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker supply a ratifierData where the decoded root is bytes32(0) and proof is empty, and if isRootRatified[maker][bytes32(0)] happens to be true (e.g., maker accidentally ratified the zero root), successfully take any offer by maker regardless of the actual offer content, because HashLib.isLeaf(bytes32(0), HashLib.hashOffer(offer), 0, []) returns true only if hashOffer(offer) == bytes32(0), which is computationally infeasible, but the zero root case should be tested? Preconditions: maker calls setIsRootRatified(maker, bytes32(0), true); taker constructs ratifierData with root=bytes32(0), leafIndex=0, proof=[]. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified(maker, bytes32(0), true) -> take(offer, abi.encode(bytes32(0), 0, []), units, taker, ...) -> isLeaf(bytes32(0), hashOffer(offer), 0, []) -> returns hashOffer(offer) == bytes32(0). Invariant tested: zero root must not be a universal bypass; isLeaf must correctly reject non-matching hashes. Scoped impact: gate bypass if zero root is exploitable. Proof idea: unit test with zero root; assert isLeaf returns false for any real offer.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer in a market with a fee-on-transfer collateral token, call take() where the seller's callback (onSell) calls supplyCollateral() with the fee-on-transfer collateral token, causing midnight to record more collateral than actually received (since safeTransferFrom delivers amount*(1-fee) but position.collateral[index] is incremented by amount), making the borrower appear more collateralized than they are and allowing them to borrow more than their actual collateral supports? Preconditions: collateral token charges 1% fee; offer is SetterRatifier-ratified; seller's callback calls supplyCollateral(market, index, amount, seller); oracle prices collateral at face value. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take() -> onSell callback -> supplyCollateral(amount) -> safeTransferFrom delivers amount*0.99 -> position.collateral[index] += amount. Invariant tested: position.collateral[index] must equal actual token balance held by midnight for that collateral. Scoped impact: bad debt creation; borrower undercollateralized relative to accounting. Proof idea: deploy FeeOnTransferCollateral; assert midnight.collateral(id, borrower, 0) > actual token balance after supplyCollateral.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified buy offer where loanToken is fee-on-transfer, cause the two sequential safeTransferFrom calls in take() (one to midnight for buyerAssets-sellerAssets, one to receiver for sellerAssets) to each lose a fee, resulting in midnight receiving (buyerAssets-sellerAssets)*(1-fee) and receiver receiving sellerAssets*(1-fee), while the accounting records full amounts, creating a double-fee loss that makes the protocol insolvent faster than a single-transfer scenario? Preconditions: loanToken charges fee on every transfer; offer.buy == true; units > 0; sellerAssets > 0. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take() -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, midnight, buyerAssets-sellerAssets) [fee taken] -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, receiver, sellerAssets) [fee taken again]. Invariant tested: total tokens received by midnight + receiver == buyerAssets (no fee loss). Scoped impact: insolvency; double fee loss per take. Proof idea: fuzz fee rate and amounts; assert loanToken.balanceOf(midnight) >= withdrawable(id) + claimableSettlementFee(loanToken) after take.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer, pass a ratifierData where proof contains a sibling hash that is the same as the leaf hash (i.e., proof[0] == HashLib.hashOffer(offer)), causing HashLib.hashNode(leafHash, proof[0]) to produce a valid parent hash for a tree where the maker ratified a root built from duplicate leaves, and does this allow the same offer to be proven at multiple leaf indices (0 and 1) in the same tree, enabling replay of the same offer fill? Preconditions: maker builds a tree with two identical leaves (same offer hash); maker ratifies the root; taker fills with leafIndex=0, then again with leafIndex=1. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> take(offer, abi.encode(root, 0, [offerHash]), units, taker) -> take(offer, abi.encode(root, 1, [offerHash]), units, taker). Invariant tested: each unique offer in a tree must be fillable at most once per consumed cap; duplicate leaves must not enable replay. Scoped impact: offer replay; overfill beyond maxUnits/maxAssets. Proof idea: construct duplicate-leaf tree; assert second take fails due to consumed cap.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer, exploit the fact that isRatified is a view function (no state change) to call it directly (not through midnight) with a spoofed msg.sender == MIDNIGHT via a proxy or CREATE2 collision, bypassing the NotMidnight check, and use the CALLBACK_SUCCESS return value to construct a fake ratification proof for use in a different context? Preconditions: attacker deploys a contract at the MIDNIGHT address (e.g., via CREATE2 before Midnight is deployed); attacker calls isRatified from that address. Call sequence: deploy FakeMidnight at MIDNIGHT address -> FakeMidnight.callIsRatified(offer, ratifierData) -> isRatified passes NotMidnight check -> returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS. Invariant tested: isRatified must only be callable by the legitimate Midnight contract; MIDNIGHT immutability must prevent spoofing. Scoped impact: gate bypass if CALLBACK_SUCCESS can be used out of context. Proof idea: test with vm.prank(MIDNIGHT); assert direct call from non-MIDNIGHT reverts.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer in a market where loanToken is a rebasing token that increases balances, cause the protocol's withdrawable accounting to undercount actual available tokens, allowing the fee claimer to drain excess tokens via claimSettlementFee or claimContinuousFee beyond what claimableSettlementFee records, or conversely, if the token rebases down, cause lenders to be unable to withdraw their full credit? Preconditions: loanToken is a rebasing token; maker has ratified root; multiple takes have occurred; token rebases after takes. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> multiple take() calls -> loanToken.rebase(+10%) -> claimSettlementFee(token, amount, receiver) where amount > claimableSettlementFee. Invariant tested: loanToken.balanceOf(midnight) >= withdrawable(id) + claimableSettlementFee(loanToken) at all times. Scoped impact: insolvency on rebase-down; excess fee extraction on rebase-up. Proof idea: deploy RebasingToken; assert balance invariant before and after rebase.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: setIsRootRatified / isRatified] Can an unprivileged attacker, who has been authorized by maker A via midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, makerA), call setIsRootRatified(makerA, root, true) to ratify a root, then call midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, false, makerA) to remove their own authorization, and then call setIsRootRatified(makerA, root, false) which now fails because attacker is no longer authorized, leaving the root permanently ratified with no way for the maker to deactivate it if the maker's key is lost? Preconditions: maker authorizes attacker; attacker ratifies malicious root; attacker removes own authorization; maker cannot deactivate root. Call sequence: maker.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, maker) -> attacker.setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) -> attacker.setIsAuthorized(attacker, false, maker) -> maker tries setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) [maker must call directly]. Invariant tested: maker must always be able to deactivate their own roots. Scoped impact: fund freeze; maker cannot cancel malicious root. Proof idea: simulate authorization removal; assert maker can still call setIsRootRatified directly.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer where the offer.callback is a contract that calls midnight.repay() with a fee-on-transfer loanToken inside onSell, cause the repay() to record debt reduction of `units` but only receive units*(1-fee) tokens from the callback, creating a shortfall in withdrawable that makes subsequent lender withdrawals revert? Preconditions: loanToken is fee-on-transfer; offer.callback calls repay(market, units, borrower, address(0), '') in onSell; midnight records withdrawable += units but receives units*(1-fee). Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take() -> onSell callback -> repay(units) -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, callback, midnight, units) delivers units*(1-fee) -> withdrawable += units. Invariant tested: withdrawable(id) <= loanToken.balanceOf(midnight) - claimableSettlementFee(loanToken). Scoped impact: insolvency; lender withdrawals revert. Proof idea: deploy FeeOnTransferToken; assert withdrawable > actual balance after callback repay.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer, pass a ratifierData where the Merkle proof is valid for a different offer (same maker, different market or tick) that is also a leaf in the same ratified tree, effectively using one ratified root to fill an offer the maker intended only for a specific market, because isRatified checks only that the offer hash is a leaf in the ratified root without any market-level scoping of the root itself? Preconditions: maker ratifies a root containing offers for market A and market B; taker uses market B's offer proof to fill market A's offer (or vice versa). Call sequence: setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) [root contains offers for both markets] -> take(offerForMarketA, proofForMarketA, units, taker, ...) [legitimate] -> take(offerForMarketB, proofForMarketB, units, taker, ...) [also legitimate, but maker may not have intended cross-market fills]. Invariant tested: ratifier approval must bind to the exact offer including market; cross-market fills must require explicit maker intent. Scoped impact: offer bug; maker's cross-market exposure exceeds intent. Proof idea: build multi-market Merkle tree; assert both offers can be filled independently.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer where loanToken has a blacklist (e.g., USDC), cause take() to revert at the safeTransferFrom step after isRatified has passed and consumed has been incremented (then rolled back by revert), and does this create a permanent DoS where the taker is blacklisted and no other taker can fill the offer because the consumed state is correctly reverted? Preconditions: loanToken has a blacklist; taker is blacklisted; payer == taker (no callback); safeTransferFrom reverts for blacklisted address. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take(offer, ratifierData, units, taker, receiver, address(0), '') -> isRatified passes -> consumed incremented -> safeTransferFrom(loanToken, taker, midnight, ...) reverts -> full revert. Invariant tested: blacklisted taker's failed take must not affect other takers; consumed must be reverted. Scoped impact: fund freeze for blacklisted taker; no impact on other takers. Proof idea: deploy BlacklistToken; assert non-blacklisted taker can still fill after blacklisted taker's revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer, exploit the ordering of operations in take() where isRatified is called before consumed is checked against maxUnits/maxAssets, to cause a scenario where isRatified succeeds but the consumed check fails, and does this revert correctly without any state being committed? Preconditions: consumed[maker][group] is already at maxUnits - 1; taker calls take with units = 2 (would exceed maxUnits); isRatified is called first and succeeds; then consumed += 2 > maxUnits causes revert. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take(offer, ratifierData, 2, taker, ...) -> isRatified passes -> consumed[maker][group] += 2 -> require(newConsumed <= maxUnits) fails -> revert. Invariant tested: consumed must not be permanently incremented if take reverts; isRatified call must not have side effects. Scoped impact: accounting corruption if consumed is partially committed. Proof idea: assert consumed unchanged after failed take due to cap exceeded.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer in a multicall, call midnight.multicall([take_calldata, setIsRootRatified_false_calldata]) where the take succeeds first and then setIsRootRatified deactivates the root, and does the multicall correctly sequence these operations without any cross-call state corruption, or can the deactivation affect the already-completed take's accounting? Preconditions: maker has authorized taker (or taker is maker); multicall contains take() followed by setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false); take completes successfully. Call sequence: midnight.multicall([abi.encodeCall(take, ...), abi.encodeCall(setIsRootRatified, (maker, root, false))]) -> take succeeds -> root deactivated. Invariant tested: multicall must not allow post-hoc state corruption of completed operations; take accounting must be final after take completes. Scoped impact: accounting corruption if multicall allows retroactive state changes. Proof idea: assert credit/debt/consumed are correct after multicall; assert root is deactivated.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer where loanToken is a token with non-standard decimals (e.g., 6 decimals like USDC or 27 decimals), cause the buyerAssets and sellerAssets calculations in take() to overflow or produce incorrect values due to mulDivDown/mulDivUp operating on amounts that are not normalized to 18 decimals, leading to accounting errors in credit, debt, or claimableSettlementFee? Preconditions: loanToken has 6 decimals; units is large (e.g., 1e6 * 1e6 = 1e12 in 6-decimal terms); offerPrice is near WAD; mulDivDown(units, buyerPrice, WAD) produces unexpected result. Call sequence: setIsRootRatified -> take(offer, ratifierData, 1e12, taker, ...) -> buyerAssets = mulDivDown(1e12, buyerPrice, WAD). Invariant tested: credit/debt accounting must be correct regardless of loanToken decimals; no overflow in asset calculations. Scoped impact: accounting corruption; insolvency. Proof idea: fuzz token decimals and units; assert credit + debt == units after take.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Non-standard ERC20 behavior, fee-on-transfer behavior, callback revert behavior, or false-return handling that breaks accounting or freezes funds'] [Function: isRatified / take] Can an unprivileged taker, using a SetterRatifier-ratified offer, pass a ratifierData where leafIndex is set to a value that is valid for the proof length (leafIndex < 2^proof.length) but corresponds to a different offer in the tree than the one being taken, causing HashLib.isLeaf to verify the wrong leaf and allowing the taker to fill an offer that was not intended to be filled with this ratifierData? Preconditions: maker ratifies a root with two offers (leftOffer at index 0, rightOffer at index 1); taker has proof for rightOffer but passes leftOffer as the offer struct with leafIndex=1.
